data.title: Yesway Restarts IPO For Debt Reduction And Continued Expansion
data.description:
Yesway Is Growing Profitably

Yesway, Inc. (YSWY) has filed to raise public capital from an IPO of its Class A common stock, per an S-1 registration statement recently filed with the SEC.

The company operates a network of convenience stores mostly in lower-tier and rural markets in the central and southern United States.

Revenue is growing moderately, but the firm is profitable and is pursuing both organic growth (new store development) and acquisition growth drivers to expand its footprint.

The IPO will likely be in demand from institutional investors, assuming management’s valuation assumptions are reasonable.

What Does Yesway Do?

Yesway is expanding its network of convenience stores and fueling stations operating via its brands Yesway and Allsup’s.

The firm previously began an IPO process in late 2021, as I wrote about here, but ended that process due to unfavorable IPO market conditions at the time.

Since then, Yesway has grown its network by a net of 16 stores, while total revenue has risen from a run rate of about $1.6 billion in 2021 to $2.7 billion in 2025, and total liabilities have also risen materially.

Operating margin has also increased since the original IPO attempt, as has operating cash flow.

The company had 419 stores in seven states as of December 31, 2025, per the map below:

Yesway sells both third-party convenience store merchandise and its house brand products.

The firm was founded in 2015 by private equity firm Brookwood and has grown substantially through 27 acquisitions while building organically (90 stores by the end of 2025).

However, if Yesway wants to expand its geographic footprint, it would have logistics constraints in that it would have to build (or buy) distribution and other capabilities to support the expanded footprint while building brand density for marketing and logistics efficiencies of scale.

The company's 2025 revenue contribution by segment is shown in the pie chart below:

For convenience store operators with fuel operations, the fuel is typically a low-margin business designed to bring in traffic, whereas the inside merchandise sales are the relatively high-margin part of the business.

In Yesway’s example, the inside merchandise is producing the majority of the gross profit, with the 2025 gross margin at 35.2%.

Compared to competitors Murphy USA and Casey’s, Yesway lands in the middle of the two in terms of gross margin, with Casey’s at around 41% and Murphy at 31%, according to recent reports.

Yesway’s co-founder, Chairman, President, and CEO is Mr. Thomas N. Trkla, who is also Chairman and CEO of Brookwood, which has invested a fair market value investment of $823 million to date.

What is Yesway’s Market?

The company operates in the convenience store and refueling market in North America, which was estimated at approximately $1 trillion in size in 2021.

The global convenience store market was expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.6% from 2022 to 2028.

By region, the Asia Pacific region is expected to grow the fastest, at a CAGR of 6.4% during the forecast period.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory of the U.S. convenience store market through 2026, by product type:

The market is expected to continue its growth path as consumers seek ready-made meals or snacks while fueling their automobiles.

By segment, the foodservice segment is expected to produce the highest CAGR through 2028, at 6.4%.

The convenience store market is highly fragmented, with greater than 50% of stores operated by single-unit owners, but the list below includes various major players:

  • Casey's General Stores, Inc. (CASY).

  • Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc. (ANCTF).

  • Murphy USA Inc. (MUSA).

  • Parkland Corporation.

  • 7-Eleven.

  • FamilyMart.

  • ARKO Petroleum Corp. (APC).

  • Major fuel companies.

Recent industry trends include the continued consolidation by larger operators, an increased focus on foodservice as a driver of traffic, and only gradual technology adoption.

The company's strengths include its geographic focus, positioning in rural & suburban markets with reduced competition, and differentiated foodservice offerings in Allsup’s.

Weaknesses include its reliance on fuel-oriented traffic, lower-density rural market dynamics, and operational complexity with its multi-format stores.

Opportunities include new store development along with M&A potential, foodservice expansion, and private-label growth and optimization via improved technology and data.

Threats are electric vehicle adoption, regulatory changes, commodity price volatility, and competitors also seeking to acquire existing operators in local regions.

Yesway’s Recent Financial Results

The company's financial results show moderate top-line revenue growth, higher gross margin, increasing net margin, and improved cash flow from operations, as the table shows here:

Yesway’s SG&A efficiency rate was 0.7x in 2025, while its core operating leverage ratio was stable at 0.35 for the last two calendar years.

Management said that same-store sales growth was up 0.4% in Q1 2025 for inside merchandise, with positive growth in 11 of the last 12 quarters, but gallons sold were down 1.9%, although this is not a predictable growth driver per se.

Yesway’s IPO Plan

The company plans to sell public investors its Class A shares in an IPO that might be as high as $300 million, excluding customary allotment to underwriters.

Class A and Class B shareholders will each receive one vote per share, while Class B shareholders will be the existing underlying operating LLC member interests.

The firm’s private equity firm sponsor, Brookwood, will remain a majority shareholder after the IPO, so the firm will be considered a ‘controlled company’ immediately post-IPO.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period after the effective date of the IPO prospectus, and the company is reserving an as-yet undetermined amount of the Class A shares for its 2026 incentive plan and ESPP.

Management said the net proceeds from the IPO will be used as follows:

We intend to cause Parent to use [an as-yet undetermined amount] of the net proceeds from the sale of LLC Interests to Yesway, Inc. to fully redeem the Redeemable Senior Preferred Membership Interests in connection with the consummation of this offering, and the remainder of the net proceeds, if any, to pay down existing indebtedness and for general corporate purposes to support the growth of the business, including new store developments. We may also cause Parent to use a portion of the net proceeds for the repayment of debt; to make cash payments to the Continuing Equity Owners pursuant to the Tax Receivable Agreement; at our option, to make cash payments to the Continuing Equity Owners upon their election to redeem any of their LLC Interests; or for the acquisition of businesses or assets that we believe are complementary to our own, although we currently have no agreements, commitments or understandings with respect to any specific acquisition.

(Source: SEC).

The company's online roadshow presentation of the IPO is not yet available.

Management said that the company is not subject to any legal proceedings or claims that would have a material effect on its operations or financial condition.

The listed underwriters include Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan, Goldman Sachs, Barclays, BMO Capital Markets, KeyBanc Capital Markets, Guggenheim Securities, and Raymond James.

Capital Allocation

As of the end of 2025, YSWY had $36.6 million in cash and equivalents and $1.1 billion in total liabilities, of which $657 million was debt.

During 2024 to 2025, Yesway improved cash conversion, reducing capex relative to cash flow, but has a thin liquidity cushion of cash to total liabilities.

The company has been substantially leveraged, with the IPO proceeds being used to reduce that high leverage, resulting in lower interest expenses and improved free cash flow post-IPO.

But, during 2024 to 2025, Yesway improved cash conversion to about 28.5% of EBITDA, reducing capex relative to cash flow, but has a thin liquidity cushion of cash to total liabilities.

So, assuming the company can succeed in obtaining sizable IPO net proceeds to make a meaningful debt reduction, it will have a cleaner balance sheet with stronger free cash flow and will be better positioned to continue to execute on its expansion plans.

Yesway Is Growing Moderately On Middling Gross Margins

Yesway has grown primarily through acquisitions and is producing moderate revenue growth, profitability, and operating cash flow.

Free cash flow for the 2025 calendar year was approximately $55 million.

The firm’s growth strategy involves focusing on larger-format stores with higher throughput located along key highways.

It also includes continued foodservice development and private-label expansion to improve margins and brand awareness.

Management will also continue to pursue opportunistic acquisitions in a fragmented market, possibly entering new geographies and leveraging its integration experience.

The convenience market is expected to continue to produce growth and consolidation opportunities for skilled and scaled consolidators like Yesway.

The company's approach has produced customer stickiness in rural markets, and management is pursuing an increasingly branded approach through its Allsup’s brand.

Risks include the ongoing transition to electric vehicles acting as a brake on ICE fuel demand, which is a major traffic driver for convenience store viability.

However, Yesway has shown a disciplined real estate strategy and capital-efficient growth model, utilizing capital-light build-to-suit opportunities wherever possible.

So, its near-term growth potential appears to be substantial, at least for the convenience store/fuel operator market.

However, Yesway’s gross margin from inside merchandise sales has been in the middle of its peer group, although rising, which is encouraging.

The company's valuation assumptions will be critical for the IPO, which will be in demand from institutional investors if it is priced reasonably.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined.

data.title: SPY: After 5 Down Weeks, Here's Why A Sharp Rebound Could Be Coming
data.description:
With the exception of some select buying opportunities, I have been bearish overall for the past few months, and I continue to be concerned about a potential recession. But I also try to adapt my views when market conditions change significantly, and I feel that right now is one of those times. All the major market indexes have plunged recently, and the S&P 500 Index (SPY) has been down for 5 weeks in a row, with losses of about 10% from recent highs. Many sectors and stocks are down even much more than this, and I believe this has started to create some buying opportunities. A 10% decline is the generally accepted definition for a market correction, and 20% is what is typically needed to declare that we are officially in a bear market. While it is possible that we are in the earlier stages of a bear market, which is what I wrote about in this article, history shows that we can expect major rallies even in a bear market.

I have been very heavy in cash for a while now, but I am still seeing some losses on my core long-term positions like Amazon (AMZN) and others as well. Losing money even on paper doesn't make me want to buy stocks now. I also don't feel like buying stocks because, like all of us, I have been reading some very negative predictions about what could happen with the war in Iran in the future. This includes talks of an "endless war" that sends oil prices up to $200 per barrel. While anything is possible, I think it makes sense to focus on the most likely outcome, which is that this oil supply shock will end, just as they always have in the past, and oil prices will plunge back to more normal levels.

Aside from my belief that the war in Iran will not keep the Strait of Hormuz closed for much longer, I see other reasons why the stock market could be poised for a potentially major rebound in the short term. Last week (on Friday), I saw what seemed to be indiscriminate selling of stocks with major losses almost across the board. This is the type of price action that often indicates that we are at or near a bottom. I also see some indicators showing that investors are extremely fearful now, and that is often a buying opportunity and a sign that a relief rally could be coming. With all of this in mind, let's take a closer look at why the market could be due for a rebound:

The Charts

As shown in the chart below, the S&P 500 Index is now just over 10% below the highs it hit earlier this year. This major index has broken below the 50-day and 200-day moving average, which is not a positive sign in terms of the chart. The only positive I see here is that this index is oversold and could be due for a rebound. Q1 earnings reports were generally solid, so the main cause of this selloff appears to be the Iran war and the subsequent surge in oil. The surge in oil prices is also causing interest rates to rise and causing concerns that the Fed might even raise rates this year to combat inflation.

The Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) is down more than 10% from recent highs, and it has also broken through key support levels such as the 50-day and 200-day moving averages. Once again, the only positive I see here is that this index appears to be oversold now and could also be due for a rebound.

The War in Iran Update

A few days ago, President Trump agreed to postpone plans to bomb Iran's energy infrastructure until April 6. This was definitely a de-escalatory move, but at the same time, there are reports that suggest more U.S. military assets are being deployed to the Middle East. In addition, some reports suggest that the Trump Administration is considering the use of ground forces to take over Kharg Island and possibly other sensitive areas. There are obviously a lot of mixed messages going on, and the stock market seems to be tiring of this. While the stock market has reacted positively to some of President Trump's de-escalatory social media posts, it seems like the market is less receptive to talk about a cease-fire and wants to see actual action that shows this war is winding down. The Iranians also seem to be sending mixed messages, and both sides are continuing to launch fresh attacks. In the past couple of days, a new and escalatory development occurred after the Houthis attacked Israel for the first time since this war started.

Based on all of this, it certainly doesn't look like the war is going to end anytime soon. I agree that we could see U.S. forces take over Kharg Island and potentially send ground forces along the coastline of the Strait of Hormuz. This could go well, just as it did with the recent military action in Venezuela, but there are many risks involved. If the U.S. captured Kharg Island, it could cut off most of Iran's oil revenue, and this could cause the regime to collapse under economic pressure, or it could surrender. However, I believe that while President Trump is willing to use these additional military deployments that could take over Kharg Island and also destroy Iran's power plants, he might not have to do so. The mere threat of this level of destruction and the potential seizure of Iran's main economic asset might be enough to force Iran into negotiations.

In addition, Iran's continued tough talk and the recent attack by the Houthis on Israel could be posturing with the hopes of putting the Iranian regime in the best possible position to negotiate an end to the war. The point of this is to suggest that even though the war seems to be continuing full speed ahead, it could very suddenly end with the announcement of a ceasefire.

Think of this as you would see two men brawling in a bar whereby the fight seems to be getting worse and worse, but then suddenly the fight ends when either both men realize ending the fight is the only way forward or when one side delivers a knockout blow. A potential U.S. takeover of Kharg Island and the destruction of power plants seems to be the final knockout blow option. Hopefully, that won't be needed, and the war, just like a fight, will be broken up soon when both parties or even observers put an end to the battle. While matters could escalate, I also believe there is a real chance of a ceasefire and peace negotiations coming. The thing is that if this does happen, it will likely be very sudden and cause a major rebound in the stock market as well as a plunge in oil prices.

Here Are Some Signs That Tell Me A Major Rebound Could Come Suddenly

1) As seen in the charts above, major market indexes are very oversold now. Stocks have now dropped for 5 weeks in a row, and this is causing me to believe that a countertrend rally could be coming soon. A 10% decline in the major market indexes is a nice round number, and at this level, we could soon start to see "buy the dip" investors get more active in the market. I also want to point out some history: corrections of between 5% and 10% typically only take about 3 months. Although corrections of 10% to 20% take about 8 months on average to recover all the losses. History also shows that the best days in the market often occur near the worst days of the market. We have had some very bad days lately, and based on history, I am expecting one or two of the best days of 2026 to also happen sometime soon.

2) Oil is in steep backwardation, which means that the oil markets believe prices are going to decline significantly. For example, even though Brent oil is currently trading for about $115 per barrel, contracts for delivery in December are priced in the $70 per barrel range. This is an indication that suggests things will likely be back to normal in the coming months, and possibly much sooner if a ceasefire comes sooner than expected. If the oil market is telling you that oil prices are going to normalize in the coming months, that is telling me that it makes sense to buy stocks now, especially ones that have been hit hardest by the surge in oil. For this reason, I have been buying airline and cruise line stocks lately.

3) The CNN Fear & Greed Index is now at 10 out of 100, which shows extreme levels of fear from investors. Even though I don't feel like buying stocks now, my personal experience has been that whenever this index is at extreme fear levels, it is usually a great time to buy stocks, even if it is just for a short-term trade. Even though I am concerned about recession risks, I believe this level of fear is suggesting a major countertrend rally is likely to come soon. When I see this index at extreme fear levels, I typically force myself to buy stocks, and that is what I plan to do this week.

Some Potential Upside Catalysts for the Stock Market

Aside from the potential end of the war and a subsequent possible decline in oil prices, I see some other potential upside catalysts for the stock market:

a) Q1 earnings season is going to get started in April. I believe earnings are going to be solid overall, and this might get investors to realize that many stocks have become disconnected from their fundamentals as the selling pressure on the stock market has started to become indiscriminate. Almost all stocks have declined in recent days, and I see this as a sign of indiscriminate selling. My point here is that Q1 earnings season will cause investors to get back to focusing on the fundamentals of each company that reports earnings, rather than focus on the war in Iran.

b) When I look at the price action after hyperscalers reported earnings for last quarter, it was very negative (even before the Iran war started), and the common theme was that these companies were announcing massive increases in AI capex spending. Now that CEOs have seen investors reaction to this level of spending and that perhaps the economy is slowing, we might also see some companies announce a reduction in these spending plans when Q1 earnings are announced. Many investors are concerned about overspending on AI capex, so a reduction in spending could cause some stocks to rally.

c) Some very high-profile companies like OpenAI and SpaceX are expected to go public later this year. Anthropic is reportedly going public in October. If the 2026 IPO market goes well, it could reignite investor interest in tech stocks.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I see the recent action with the Iran war as being part of posturing for a potential ceasefire and to position each side for the best positioning before potential negotiations. However, I also believe this could also get much worse before it gets better, especially if any potential military action regarding Kharg Island does not go well. Since things could get worse before potentially getting better, I am only buying stocks in stages, which allows me to dollar cost average in the event that prices go even lower.

In Summary

Even though I have been generally bearish and raising cash for the past few months, I can't ignore that stocks have dropped more than 10% and are now oversold. I also can't ignore that my experience has shown it pays off to buy stocks when the CNN Fear & Greed Index is at extreme fear levels, as it is now. A 5-week decline in stocks also makes me expect that a countertrend rally could be coming soon. Finally, I see the current market plunge as being caused by an oil supply shock. History shows that oil supply shocks never last for long. Finally, the steep backwardation in the oil market is also telling me I should buy stocks and sell oil-related assets.

data.title: Micron: Momentum Fatigue Triggers A Cheap, Dip-Buying Opportunity
data.description:
I previously covered Micron Technology (MU) in December 2025, with my reiterated Buy rating attributed to their AI beneficiary status during the multi-year cloud supercycle and the robust hyperscaler demand for memory/storage offerings.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the MU stock here, thanks to the improved risk/reward and the cheaper valuations from the recent meltdown, significantly aided by their ability to sustainably fund their growth ambitions through the growing cash flows/rich balance sheet.

MU's Bull Trap Meets Robust Fundamentals

Since my last Buy rating, MU has gone ahead and charted a new high of $471s by mid-March 2026 before losing part of those gains to retest the $355s by the time of writing—with a similar correction also observed in its memory/semiconductor peers in varying degrees.

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to the ongoing market rotation, worsened by the macroeconomic uncertainty from the higher inflationary pressures and the ongoing Iran conflict, with it seemingly bringing forth a replay of the November 2021 selloff.

1. Capex Pessimism

For now, I am of the opinion that MU's pullback has been a boon, since the stock's YTD rally through February 2026 has triggered the notable divergence against my prior Buy Zones of $240s and/or the 50-day moving averages offered in the last article.

This is especially since the memory company has joined the hyperscalers' 'elevated capex party,' with it perhaps contributing to the market's pessimism surrounding the debt-ridden data center capex trends and the stock's consequent selloff by -24.5% post FQ2'26 earnings call.

For reference, MU has already offered an FY2026 capex guidance of over $25B (+81.1% YoY) and the preliminary FY2027 capex guidance of $35B (+40% YoY), with these numbers well exceeding the last five years' cumulative capex of $51.5B.

For one, I am of the opinion that the memory company remains well capitalized to embark on the higher capex trends, with the robust YTD net cash provided by operating activities growth to $20.31B (+182.8% YoY) against the YTD capex of $11.77B (+62.1% YoY) triggering the still rich YTD Free Cash Flow generation of $8.54B (compared to a year ago at -$0.08B).

This is on top of the richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $4.45B in FQ2'26 (+172.4% YoY) along with the healthy net debt to annualized H1'26 adj. EBITDA ratio of -0.11x (given the higher cash position) compared to the Semiconductor Equipment & Materials leverage average of 0.02x.

As a result, I believe that MU's higher capex is likely to be well supported by the richer cash flows and the healthier balance sheet, all of which signal the sustainability of its capacity growth prospects.

2. Robust Demand

An important metric that investors may use to measure the demand profile for MU's memory offerings is the adj. gross profit margins—one that has been expanding to 74.9% in FQ2'26 (+37 points YoY/+28 from FY2019 levels of 46.9%), with it signaling their improving pricing power at a time of supply crunch.

The same has been observed in the robust memory demand across all of its operating segments through their top/bottom-line expansions on a QoQ/YoY basis.

Readers may also want to note MU's tailwinds from the upcoming exit of the crucial consumer business, with FQ3'26 likely to bring forth a higher revenue concentration in the cloud and data center segment, as tech giants are also "paying a premium for multiyear contracts that guarantee supply in the future" aided by their new multi-year agreement:

This development is unsurprising indeed, since the insatiable compute demand has already contributed to the four leading hyperscalers "now expecting combined spending of close to $700 billion" on data center-related capex entering 2026.

As a result of the higher pricing trends, the durable multi-year cloud spending trends, and the market analysts' projections of the global data center capacity expected to "nearly double from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030," I am of the opinion that MU's prospects as a leading memory player remain extremely robust moving forward.

These developments are also why I am of the opinion that the stock's recent correction is merely attributed to the cooling AI sentiments instead of fundamentals, as the world's leading foundry, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), is also "reaching capacity limits, and the industry is facing broader supply chain constraints amid surging demand for AI chips."

3. Profitable Growth Trends Are Cheaply Valued

MU has confirmed the resilience of its profitable growth prospects through the outsized FQ3'26 revenue guidance of $33.5B (+260.2% YoY) and adj. gross margin guidance of 81% (+42 points YoY), with it apparent that the AI/memory party is still going strong despite the recent selloff.

The same has been embedded in the consensus' raised forward estimates, with the memory company expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth prospect at a CAGR of +60.3%/+110.8% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +11.8%/+24% and a 10Y CAGR of +8.7%/+11.9%, respectively.

Thanks to the steep -24.5% meltdown from the recent highs and the consistently raised consensus forward estimates, it is unsurprising that MU is even cheaper at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 6.60x, down from the 1Y mean of 10.77x, the 5Y mean of 74.61x, the 10Y mean of 74.61x, and the sector median of 21.57x.

The same conclusion may also be derived from my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.05x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 6.60x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +110.8%, compared to the 5Y mean of 3.10x, the 10Y mean of 6.26x, and the sector median of 1.28x.

When comparing to MU's memory/storage/semiconductor peers, including:

  • DRAM and NAND - Samsung (SSNLF) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 7.21x/3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.11x (using the same methodology)
  • DRAM and NAND - SK hynix at 4.70x/0.07x,
  • NAND - Sandisk (SNDK) at 14.98x/0.07x,
  • HDD - Seagate (STX) at 29.09x/0.62x,
  • general AI - Nvidia (NVDA) at 20.64x/0.50x,
  • general AI - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 30.70x/0.59x,
  • custom AI - Broadcom (AVGO) at 27.33x/0.57x, and
  • custom AI - Marvell (MRVL) at 25.55x/0.67x, respectively,

it is apparent that MU's highly profitable growth prospect is even more compelling after the recent correction, with it unlocking a great dip-buying opportunity for those who have been waiting on the sidelines.

MU Is An Even Better Buy Here

For now, MU's rare triple top pattern has materialized after the recent correction, with the stock losing nearly all of its YTD gains while trading below the prior YTD floor of $370s.

MU now boasts the more reasonable technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes, with it also bringing the stock below my fair value estimates of $525.50, based on the annualized FQ2'26 adj. EPS performance of $48.80 (+155.2% QoQ/+682% YoY) and the 1Y P/E mean of 10.77x (cheap compared to the sector median of 21x).

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $77.66, there appears to be an excellent more than doubling upside potential to my long-term price target of $836.30 as well, with it apparent that the selloff has been a boon.

As a result of their ability to capitalize on the ongoing supply crunch/multi-year cloud supercycle while sustainably funding their growth ambitions through the growing cash flows/rich balance sheet, along with the pullback already triggering an improved risk/reward for those looking to tap into the AI boom, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the MU stock here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Where Will The Billions Of Nuclear Funding Dollars Go?
data.description:
By Steffan Szumowski

Investors have taken notice of the eye-popping federal commitments to new nuclear capacity in the U.S. in recent months. Headlines have focused on massive reactor deployment partnerships and loan authority in the hundreds of billions. While reactor developers certainly stand to benefit, a closer look reveals that the majority of this capital will flow through the broader nuclear value chain to fuel suppliers, component manufacturers, construction firms, and service providers. Diversified exposure across the full supply chain often captures more of the upside than any single reactor play alone.

The $80 Billion Westinghouse Partnership

In late October 2025, the U.S. government entered a strategic partnership with Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) and Cameco Corporation (CCJ) to accelerate deployment of Westinghouse’s AP1000 reactor. At the center of the deal is $80 billion for constructing 10 reactors across the U.S. The government committed to arranging financing, streamlining permitting, and supporting long-lead procurement. CCJ, which owns 49% of Westinghouse alongside Brookfield’s 51% stake, brings not only reactor expertise but also critical fuel supply capabilities to the table.

The announcement was celebrated as a win for Westinghouse and its owners, but it should be understood that the majority of this funding will be spent long before a single watt is generated from any of these new reactors. Some of the components of the new plants will require significant lead time with fabrication and forging. Site evaluation and preparation will take months or years as well.

The $40 Billion U.S.-Japan SMR Initiative

More recently, President Trump and Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi announced up to $40 billion in joint investment for GE Vernova (GEV) and Hitachi to deploy BWRX-300 small modular reactors in Tennessee and Alabama. The deal is part of a larger bilateral energy package and underscores growing international collaboration on advanced nuclear energy.

Like the Westinghouse partnership, the capital commitment extends well beyond the SMR vendor. GE Vernova’s role as reactor designer is important, but the project will require extensive supply chain support: specialized forgings, valves, pumps, instrumentation, and construction services. Early procurement of long lead items and fuel will represent meaningful revenue streams for companies positioned across the value chain.

Hundreds of Billions More Through the Office of Energy Dominance Financing

Last year, Energy Secretary Chris Wright highlighted the Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office, now the Office of Energy Dominance Financing (EDF), as the largest single source of federal financing for new nuclear. The EDF carries hundreds of billions in lending authority through loan guarantees and direct loans. These are financing vehicles specifically designed to help projects that struggle to secure traditional bank loans. Secretary Wright stated plainly that “by far the biggest use of those dollars will be for nuclear power plants to get those first plants built.”

This office has already deployed several billions in funding to support the nuclear power plant restart in Michigan, the restart of the Three Mile Island facility, and the uprating of existing nuclear plants owned by Southern Company.

Where is the Money Going?

The vast majority of the funding will be spent on the fuel and supply chain:

  • Uranium must be mined, processed, and fabricated for new fuel.
  • Front-end engineering studies must be conducted and evaluated.
  • Site preparation and construction will require significant amounts of concrete, steel, time and effort.
  • Tens of billions of dollars will be spent on systems and equipment for the new reactors, including instrumentation, piping, pumps, valves, and control systems.

Investors can expect to see a similar list of companies for the development of almost any reactor plant in the U.S.:

  • Mining companies such as Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC)
  • Engineering firms such as Fluor (FLR) and Ameresco (AMRC)
  • Equipment manufacturers such as Mirion Technologies (MIR), Curtiss-Wright (CW), BWX Technologies (BWXT), and Flowserve (FLS)

CCJ, GEV, BWXT, FLR, AMRC, UEC, MIR, CW, BWXT, and FLS are all constituents of the VettaFi Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX) as shown below. NUKZX includes companies across the nuclear value chain, from fuel to utilities. NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).
